title: Genmab — Hematology — Knowledge Doc
html:
Market Context: EPKINLY (Epcoritamab) in DLBCL & Follicular Lymphoma

Product: EPKINLY (epcoritamab-bysp) Manufacturer: Genmab A/S (co-commercialized with AbbVie in US and Japan) Mechanism: CD20xCD3 T-cell engaging bispecific antibody, subcutaneous Indications: R/R DLBCL (3L+, approved May 2023); R/R FL monotherapy (2L+, accelerated approval Jun 2024, converted to full approval Nov 2025); R/R FL combination with R2 (2L+, approved Nov 2025) Document scope: Product-level competitive/clinical landscape (not wave-specific) Last updated: April 2026

1. Disease Landscape

1.1 Epidemiology

Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma (DLBCL):

  • Most common aggressive non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL), representing ~25-30% of all NHL cases
  • ~28,000 new cases/year in the US
  • Median age at diagnosis: ~65 years
  • Frontline R-CHOP cures ~60% of patients; the remaining ~40% relapse or are refractory
  • 5-year survival for R/R DLBCL after second-line failure: historically <25% (improving with novel therapies)

Follicular Lymphoma (FL):

  • Most common indolent NHL, representing ~20% of all NHL
  • ~15,000 new cases/year in the US
  • Median age at diagnosis: ~60 years
  • Generally incurable but follows a relapsing/remitting course; median OS >15 years with modern therapy
  • After 2+ lines, outcomes deteriorate significantly; unmet need grows with each subsequent line

1.2 Disease Biology

Key biological concepts relevant to the competitive landscape:

  • CD20: Surface antigen expressed on most B-cell lymphomas (DLBCL and FL). Target for rituximab (anti-CD20 mAb), and the B-cell binding arm of bispecific antibodies (epcoritamab, glofitamab, mosunetuzumab, odronextamab)
  • CD3: Surface receptor on T cells. The T-cell binding arm of bispecific antibodies redirects T cells to kill CD20+ tumor cells — this is the mechanism of action for all approved bispecifics in lymphoma
  • Bispecific antibody MOA: Epcoritamab simultaneously binds CD20 on B-cell lymphoma cells and CD3 on T cells, inducing immediate T-cell engagement and killing of CD20+ cells. Unlike CAR-T, no manufacturing or leukapheresis required — it is an "off-the-shelf" therapy
  • CRS (cytokine release syndrome): A class effect of T-cell engaging therapies. Managed via step-up dosing. Epcoritamab's CRS is predominantly low-grade and predictable
  • Subcutaneous administration: Epcoritamab is the first and only SubQ bispecific antibody in lymphoma. Enables outpatient administration after step-up period, a key differentiation vs IV bispecifics and CAR-T

1.3 Standard Treatment Algorithm

Newly Diagnosed DLBCL
  |
  ├── First-line: R-CHOP (rituximab + cyclophosphamide + doxorubicin + vincristine + prednisone)
  |     └── Pola-R-CHP (POLIVY + R-CHP) gaining share per POLARIX data
  |
  ├── ~60% cured; ~40% relapse/refractory
  |
  v
First Relapse (2L)
  |
  ├── Transplant-eligible: Salvage chemo → ASCT
  |     └── CAR-T in 2L: Breyanzi (TRANSFORM), Yescarta (ZUMA-7) — now standard for many 2L patients
  |
  ├── Transplant-ineligible: Chemoimmunotherapy (R-GemOx, Pola-BR, etc.)
  |     └── Glofitamab + GemOx (STARGLO) emerging as 2L option
  |     └── EPCORE DLBCL-1: epcoritamab monotherapy showed PFS benefit (HR 0.74) vs chemo in 2L+ R/R
  |
  v
Third Line and Beyond (3L+)
  |
  └── Key battleground for bispecifics and CAR-T:
        ├── EPKINLY (epcoritamab) — CD20xCD3 bispecific, SubQ, treat-to-progression
        ├── COLUMVI (glofitamab) — CD20xCD3 bispecific, IV, fixed-duration (~8.5 months)
        ├── Mosunetuzumab — CD20xCD3 bispecific (approved in FL; investigational in DLBCL)
        ├── CAR-T: Yescarta (axi-cel), Breyanzi (liso-cel), Kymriah (tisa-cel)
        ├── POLIVY (polatuzumab vedotin) — CD79b-targeted ADC
        └── MONJUVI (tafasitamab) + lenalidomide

Relapsed/Refractory Follicular Lymphoma
  |
  ├── 2L+: R2 (rituximab + lenalidomide) — established SOC
  |     └── EPKINLY + R2 (EPCORE FL-1): ORR/CR 96%/~75%, significantly superior PFS vs R2 alone
  |     └── Mosunetuzumab monotherapy (approved 3L+ FL)
  |     └── Breyanzi (CAR-T, approved R/R FL)
  |
  ├── 3L+: EPKINLY monotherapy (full approval, Nov 2025)
  |     └── Mosunetuzumab monotherapy (ORR 80%, CR 60% in GO29781)
  |
  └── Emerging: CELESTIMO (mosunetuzumab + lenalidomide vs R-len in R/R FL)

1.4 Unmet Needs

  1. Cure in R/R DLBCL remains elusive: Even with bispecifics and CAR-T, most 3L+ patients eventually relapse. Durable CRs are achievable but not universal
  2. CAR-T access bottleneck: 4-6 week manufacturing time, limited certified treatment centers, referral delays. Creates a natural gap that bispecifics can fill
  3. Community oncology access: CAR-T is predominantly academic-center based. Bispecifics (especially SubQ epcoritamab) have greater potential for community adoption, but step-up dosing still requires initial hospitalization/monitoring
  4. 2L treatment optimization: For transplant-ineligible 2L patients, no clear standard exists. Bispecifics, CAR-T, and chemoimmunotherapy all compete. Sequencing decisions are physician-dependent
  5. FL combination therapy: R2 is established but responses are finite. Adding a bispecific (epcoritamab + R2) appears to deepen and extend responses — this is a new paradigm
  6. Bispecific vs CAR-T debate: No head-to-head data. Treat-to-progression (epcoritamab) vs one-time treatment (CAR-T) is an unresolved clinical and economic question. Fixed-duration glofitamab offers a middle ground
  7. Post-bispecific treatment: What to do after bispecific failure is poorly defined. CAR-T after bispecific exposure? Different target bispecific? This is an emerging unmet need

2. Product Profiles

2.1 EPKINLY (epcoritamab-bysp) — Genmab/AbbVie

Mechanism of Action / Delivery: Epcoritamab is a CD20xCD3 T-cell engaging bispecific antibody administered subcutaneously. It redirects T cells to kill CD20-expressing B-cell lymphoma cells through T-cell engagement and activation. The SubQ route enables outpatient administration after the step-up dosing period and is a key competitive differentiator.

Approved Indications (as of April 2026):

  1. R/R DLBCL, NOS (including DLBCL arising from indolent lymphoma) and HGBL after 2+ lines of systemic therapy (May 2023, accelerated approval)
  2. R/R FL after 2+ lines of systemic therapy (Jun 2024, accelerated; converted to full approval Nov 2025)
  3. R/R FL after 1+ line of systemic therapy in combination with R2 (Nov 2025, full approval based on EPCORE FL-1)

Key Clinical Data:

  • EPCORE NHL-1 (3L+ DLBCL): ORR 63%, CR 39%; mDOR 15.6 months; 3-year DoCR 36.1 months. In CAR-T exposed patients: ORR 52%, CR 35%; in CAR-T refractory: ORR 42%, CR 28%
  • EPCORE FL-1 (2L+ FL combination): Epcoritamab + R2 vs R2: ORR/CR 96%/~75% vs ~80%/~34%; significantly superior PFS; ~3 out of 4 patients achieved CR. Category 1 Preferred in NCCN guidelines
  • EPCORE DLBCL-1 (2L+ R/R DLBCL, Phase 3, Jan 2026): Epcoritamab monotherapy vs investigator's choice chemo: PFS improved (HR 0.74, 95% CI 0.60-0.92); CRR, DoR, and time to next treatment improved. OS not statistically significant (HR 0.96). This was a mixed result — PFS win but OS miss
  • EPCORE NHL-2 Arm 1 (1L DLBCL, Phase 1/2): Epcoritamab + R-CHOP: ORR 98%, CR 85%; 92% remained in CR at 2 years, 74% at 3 years

Safety Profile:

  • CRS: Predominantly low-grade, predictable, manageable with 3-step-up dosing
  • No ICANS (immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome) reported with 3-step-up dosing in FL
  • Most common AEs: CRS, fatigue, injection site reactions, musculoskeletal pain
  • Treat-to-progression dosing: administered once every 4 weeks after cycle 3 in DLBCL; up to 12 cycles in FL combination

Revenue:

  • FY2025 global net sales: $468M (+67% YoY)
  • Genmab net product sales (US + Japan): $379M (+50%)
  • Run-rate trajectory suggests ~$600-700M+ potential for FY2026

Commercial Structure:

  • Genmab originated the DuoBody platform and epcoritamab
  • AbbVie co-development and co-commercialization in US and Japan (50/50 cost sharing; AbbVie received $750M upfront + up to $3.15B milestones)
  • AbbVie's sales force promotes alongside Genmab; AbbVie call plan targets segmented by DLBCL/FL
  • Genmab records product sales; AbbVie profit-sharing recognized as cost of goods (~$46M/quarter in Q3 2025)

2.2 COLUMVI (glofitamab-gxbm) — Roche/Genentech

Mechanism / Delivery: CD20xCD3 bispecific antibody, IV administration, fixed-duration treatment (~8.5 months, 12 cycles).

Approved Indication: R/R DLBCL, NOS or LBCL arising from FL, after 2+ lines of systemic therapy (Jun 2023). Also approved in 2L+ DLBCL based on STARGLO.

Key Data:

  • NP30179 (3L+ DLBCL): ORR 56%, CR 43%; mDOR 18.4 months; mDOCR not reached. Fixed-duration is a key differentiator — patients complete therapy
  • STARGLO (2L+ DLBCL, Phase 3): Glofitamab + GemOx vs R-GemOx: OS significantly improved (median 25.5 vs 12.9 months, HR 0.59); ORR 56% vs 43%, CR 58.5% vs 25.3%. This is the strongest 2L dataset among bispecifics in DLBCL
  • SKYGLO (1L DLBCL, Phase 3): Glofitamab + Pola-R-CHP vs Pola-R-CHP. Ongoing — data anticipated
  • CRS: Any grade 70%; Grade 1 in 52%, Grade 2 in 14%, Grade 3 in 2.8%, Grade 4 in 1.4% — higher overall CRS rate than epcoritamab but also mostly low-grade

Competitive Position:

  • Fixed-duration is attractive for patients and physicians who prefer a defined treatment endpoint
  • IV administration is a disadvantage vs epcoritamab's SubQ
  • STARGLO 2L OS data (HR 0.59) is the strongest Phase 3 outcome data for any bispecific in DLBCL — a direct competitive threat to epcoritamab's 2L positioning

2.3 Mosunetuzumab (Lunsumio) — Roche/Genentech

Mechanism / Delivery: CD20xCD3 bispecific antibody, IV administration, fixed-duration.

Approved Indication: R/R FL after 2+ lines of systemic therapy (Dec 2022).

Key Data:

  • GO29781 (R/R FL): ORR 80%, CR 60%; mDOR 35.9 months at 37.4-month follow-up. CRS 39%, <1% led to discontinuation
  • Not approved for DLBCL but being investigated in combinations
  • CELESTIMO (Phase 3): Mosunetuzumab + lenalidomide vs R-lenalidomide in R/R FL. Direct competitor to epcoritamab + R2

Competitive Position:

  • First bispecific approved in FL (before epcoritamab), but as monotherapy only
  • FL monotherapy data is strong (CR 60%) but inferior to epcoritamab + R2 combo (CR ~75%)
  • CELESTIMO is the key head-to-head threat in FL — if mosunetuzumab + lenalidomide matches epcoritamab + R2, Roche maintains competitive parity
  • Also developing SubQ formulation (NCT04246086)

2.4 CAR-T Therapies

Yescarta (axicabtagene ciloleucel) — Gilead/Kite:

  • CD19-directed CAR-T; approved in 2L+ DLBCL (ZUMA-7) and 3L+ LBCL
  • One-time treatment; deepest responses in fit patients
  • In the DLBCL PET tracker (Q4 2023), YESCARTA had the highest likelihood to increase prescription (81%), well above EPKINLY (67%)

Breyanzi (lisocabtagene maraleucel) — BMS:

  • CD19-directed CAR-T; approved in 2L+ LBCL (TRANSFORM) and R/R FL (TRANSCEND FL)
  • Defined composition (CD4:CD8 ratio) with 4-1BB costimulatory domain
  • TRANSCEND FL: ORR 96%, CR 73% in R/R FL — directly competitive with epcoritamab + R2 in FL
  • MSL study: BMS MSLs notably led in "trusted partner" perception

Kymriah (tisagenlecleucel) — Novartis:

  • CD19-directed CAR-T; approved in 3L+ DLBCL
  • Declining market share; less competitive vs Yescarta/Breyanzi in current landscape

CAR-T Competitive Dynamics:

  • One-time treatment vs epcoritamab's continuous therapy is the fundamental value proposition debate
  • CAR-T manufacturing bottleneck (4-6 weeks) creates natural bispecific use case
  • Epcoritamab shows activity post-CAR-T (ORR 52% in CAR-T exposed), positioning it as a viable sequence after CAR-T failure
  • CAR-T requires specialized centers; bispecifics are more community-accessible

2.5 Other Competitors

POLIVY (polatuzumab vedotin) — Roche/Genentech:

  • CD79b-targeted ADC; approved in 1L DLBCL (Pola-R-CHP, POLARIX) and R/R DLBCL (with BR)
  • Dominant in frontline; not a direct competitor in 3L+ but contributes to share of voice
  • In the PET tracker, POLIVY led in personal promotion reach (56%) and unaided awareness (43%)

Odronextamab — Regeneron:

  • CD20xCD3 bispecific; EMA-approved for R/R DLBCL and FL (2L+); NOT FDA-approved
  • ORR 52%, CR 31% in R/R DLBCL (ELM-2)
  • Absent from US competitive dynamics but may eventually enter

Lenalidomide (Revlimid) — BMS:

  • Backbone of R2 combination in FL; also used with mosunetuzumab in CELESTIMO
  • Epcoritamab + R2 approval builds on lenalidomide's established role in FL

3. Competitive Landscape Summary

3.1 DLBCL 3L+ Market Share of Voice (ZoomRx PET Q4 2023)

Based on the Promotional Effectiveness Tracker:

  • POLIVY leads in personal promotion reach (56%) and unaided awareness (43%)
  • EPKINLY and COLUMVI tied in personal promotion reach (38%), but EPKINLY leads in unaided awareness (24% vs 15%)
  • EPKINLY overall call quality: 75% (competitive with COLUMVI at 76%)
  • EPKINLY likelihood to increase prescription: 67% (improved from prior quarter)
  • YESCARTA had highest prescribing intent (81%) — CAR-T's strength in conversion
  • EPKINLY leads COLUMVI in message association and message correct attribution

3.2 MSL Competitive Dynamics (ZoomRx MSL Wave 1, 2025)

Based on the MSL Impact Assessment:

  • Epcoritamab MSL reach was significantly higher vs most competitors; driven by community hospital reach
  • Epcoritamab MSL share of voice narrowly edged out competitors with leading reach and competitive frequency
  • AbbVie/Genmab MSLs drove the strongest manufacturer perceptions; rated most often as "Best in Class"
  • AbbVie/Genmab MSL effectiveness average: 74% (competitive with glofitamab at 75%, ahead of mosunetuzumab at 66%)
  • ~60% of epcoritamab MSL interactions were high-impact (leading vs competitors)
  • AbbVie/Genmab MSLs excelled in FL scientific information discussion; ranked mid-pack for DLBCL
  • BMS MSLs notably led in "trusted partner" perception
  • Post-MSL interaction, HCPs most often discussed epcoritamab info with peers; significantly more independent research and event attendance for epcoritamab vs competitors

3.3 Key Competitive Differentiators

Feature EPKINLY COLUMVI Mosunetuzumab CAR-T (Yescarta/Breyanzi)
Route SubQ IV IV (SubQ in dev) IV (leukapheresis + infusion)
Duration Treat-to-progression Fixed (~8.5 mo) Fixed One-time
CRS rate Low-grade, predictable 70% any grade 39% Variable (higher severity)
3L+ DLBCL ORR 63% 56% Not approved 52-83% (varies)
FL indication 2L+ combo + 3L+ mono No 3L+ mono 3L+ (Breyanzi)
Community access Higher (SubQ) Moderate Moderate Low (academic centers)
Off-the-shelf Yes Yes Yes No (4-6 wk manufacturing)

4. Pipeline & Upcoming Catalysts

4.1 Epcoritamab Pipeline

Trial Setting Phase Status Expected Readout
EPCORE DLBCL-2 1L DLBCL (epcoritamab + R-CHOP vs R-CHOP) Phase 3 Enrolling (~900 pts) 2026 (frontline)
EPCORE DLBCL-1 2L+ R/R DLBCL (monotherapy vs chemo) Phase 3 Topline Jan 2026 (PFS+, OS-) Data presentation at medical meeting
EPCORE NHL-2 Various combinations (1L DLBCL, FL) Phase 1/2 Multiple arms ongoing Ongoing
EPCORE FL-1 2L+ FL (epcoritamab + R2 vs R2) Phase 3 Completed — basis for Nov 2025 approval Approved

4.2 Competitor Pipeline

Trial Agent Setting Status
STARGLO Glofitamab + GemOx 2L+ DLBCL Completed — approved
SKYGLO Glofitamab + Pola-R-CHP 1L DLBCL Ongoing — key 1L competitor
CELESTIMO Mosunetuzumab + lenalidomide R/R FL Ongoing — direct FL threat
MorningSun SC mosunetuzumab Untreated FL Phase 2
SUNMO Mosunetuzumab + polatuzumab R/R aggressive B-NHL Ongoing

4.3 Genmab Broader Pipeline

Rina-S (rinatabart sesutecan): Genmab's next major pipeline asset; ADC targeting FRalpha. Phase 3 in platinum-resistant ovarian cancer (RAINFOL-02), endometrial cancer (RAINFOL-03), and platinum-sensitive ovarian cancer maintenance (RAINFOL-04, starting Jan 2026). FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation in advanced endometrial cancer. First registrational readout expected 2026 with potential 2027 launch. Not in hematology but critical to Genmab's overall portfolio diversification.

5. Genmab/AbbVie Alliance Structure

Partnership origin: June 2020 — broad oncology collaboration Scope: Joint development and co-commercialization of epcoritamab (and two other bispecific candidates) in US and Japan Economics:

  • AbbVie upfront: $750M
  • Milestone potential: up to $3.15B
  • Cost sharing: 50/50 for epcoritamab development and commercialization costs
  • Genmab books revenue; AbbVie profit-sharing as COGS (~$46M/quarter in Q3 2025)
  • Outside US/Japan: Genmab retains commercialization rights

Commercial implications:

  • Dual sales force (AbbVie reps + Genmab MSLs) provides broader reach
  • AbbVie call plan segments by DLBCL/FL designation
  • AbbVie brings oncology commercial infrastructure; Genmab brings innovation and hematology depth
  • The Q4 2023 PET tracker showed coordinated promotional activity across both partners' target lists

6. Key Metrics to Track

  1. Epkinly quarterly revenue trajectory — FY2025 was $468M (+67%); FY2026 guidance embedded in Genmab's $4.1-4.4B overall revenue target
  2. FL combination adoption — post-Nov 2025 approval, how rapidly does epcoritamab + R2 become standard 2L FL therapy?
  3. EPCORE DLBCL-2 readout — the 1L DLBCL prize. If epcoritamab + R-CHOP beats R-CHOP, it transforms the market
  4. EPCORE DLBCL-1 full data presentation — PFS was positive but OS was not; regulatory and commercial implications TBD
  5. CELESTIMO readout — if mosunetuzumab + lenalidomide matches epcoritamab + R2 in FL, Roche recaptures parity
  6. Community adoption rate — SubQ advantage should drive community penetration; track MSL reach in community settings
  7. Post-CAR-T sequencing patterns — epcoritamab's activity in CAR-T exposed patients is a unique selling point

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home

headings.text:
1. Disease Landscape
1.1 Epidemiology
Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma (DLBCL):

Most common aggressive non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL), representing ~25-30% of all NHL cases
~28,000 new cases/year in the US
Median age at diagnosis: ~65 years
Frontline R-CHOP cures ~60% of patients; the remaining ~40% relapse or are refractory
5-year survival for R/R DLBCL after second-line failure: historically <25% (improving with novel therapies)

Follicular Lymphoma (FL):

Most common indolent NHL, representing ~20% of all NHL
~15,000 new cases/year in the US
Median age at diagnosis: ~60 years
Generally incurable but follows a relapsing/remitting course; median OS >15 years with modern therapy
After 2+ lines, outcomes deteriorate significantly; unmet need grows with each subsequent line

1.2 Disease Biology
Key biological concepts relevant to the competitive landscape:

CD20: Surface antigen expressed on most B-cell lymphomas (DLBCL and FL). Target for rituximab (anti-CD20 mAb), and the B-cell binding arm of bispecific antibodies (epcoritamab, glofitamab, mosunetuzumab, odronextamab)
CD3: Surface receptor on T cells. The T-cell binding arm of bispecific antibodies redirects T cells to kill CD20+ tumor cells — this is the mechanism of action for all approved bispecifics in lymphoma
Bispecific antibody MOA: Epcoritamab simultaneously binds CD20 on B-cell lymphoma cells and CD3 on T cells, inducing immediate T-cell engagement and killing of CD20+ cells. Unlike CAR-T, no manufacturing or leukapheresis required — it is an "off-the-shelf" therapy
CRS (cytokine release syndrome): A class effect of T-cell engaging therapies. Managed via step-up dosing. Epcoritamab's CRS is predominantly low-grade and predictable
Subcutaneous administration: Epcoritamab is the first and only SubQ bispecific antibody in lymphoma. Enables outpatient administration after step-up period, a key differentiation vs IV bispecifics and CAR-T

1.3 Standard Treatment Algorithm
Newly Diagnosed DLBCL
  |
  ├── First-line: R-CHOP (rituximab + cyclophosphamide + doxorubicin + vincristine + prednisone)
  |     └── Pola-R-CHP (POLIVY + R-CHP) gaining share per POLARIX data
  |
  ├── ~60% cured; ~40% relapse/refractory
  |
  v
First Relapse (2L)
  |
  ├── Transplant-eligible: Salvage chemo → ASCT
  |     └── CAR-T in 2L: Breyanzi (TRANSFORM), Yescarta (ZUMA-7) — now standard for many 2L patients
  |
  ├── Transplant-ineligible: Chemoimmunotherapy (R-GemOx, Pola-BR, etc.)
  |     └── Glofitamab + GemOx (STARGLO) emerging as 2L option
  |     └── EPCORE DLBCL-1: epcoritamab monotherapy showed PFS benefit (HR 0.74) vs chemo in 2L+ R/R
  |
  v
Third Line and Beyond (3L+)
  |
  └── Key battleground for bispecifics and CAR-T:
        ├── EPKINLY (epcoritamab) — CD20xCD3 bispecific, SubQ, treat-to-progression
        ├── COLUMVI (glofitamab) — CD20xCD3 bispecific, IV, fixed-duration (~8.5 months)
        ├── Mosunetuzumab — CD20xCD3 bispecific (approved in FL; investigational in DLBCL)
        ├── CAR-T: Yescarta (axi-cel), Breyanzi (liso-cel), Kymriah (tisa-cel)
        ├── POLIVY (polatuzumab vedotin) — CD79b-targeted ADC
        └── MONJUVI (tafasitamab) + lenalidomide

Relapsed/Refractory Follicular Lymphoma
  |
  ├── 2L+: R2 (rituximab + lenalidomide) — established SOC
  |     └── EPKINLY + R2 (EPCORE FL-1): ORR/CR 96%/~75%, significantly superior PFS vs R2 alone
  |     └── Mosunetuzumab monotherapy (approved 3L+ FL)
  |     └── Breyanzi (CAR-T, approved R/R FL)
  |
  ├── 3L+: EPKINLY monotherapy (full approval, Nov 2025)
  |     └── Mosunetuzumab monotherapy (ORR 80%, CR 60% in GO29781)
  |
  └── Emerging: CELESTIMO (mosunetuzumab + lenalidomide vs R-len in R/R FL)

1.4 Unmet Needs

Cure in R/R DLBCL remains elusive: Even with bispecifics and CAR-T, most 3L+ patients eventually relapse. Durable CRs are achievable but not universal
CAR-T access bottleneck: 4-6 week manufacturing time, limited certified treatment centers, referral delays. Creates a natural gap that bispecifics can fill
Community oncology access: CAR-T is predominantly academic-center based. Bispecifics (especially SubQ epcoritamab) have greater potential for community adoption, but step-up dosing still requires initial hospitalization/monitoring
2L treatment optimization: For transplant-ineligible 2L patients, no clear standard exists. Bispecifics, CAR-T, and chemoimmunotherapy all compete. Sequencing decisions are physician-dependent
FL combination therapy: R2 is established but responses are finite. Adding a bispecific (epcoritamab + R2) appears to deepen and extend responses — this is a new paradigm
Bispecific vs CAR-T debate: No head-to-head data. Treat-to-progression (epcoritamab) vs one-time treatment (CAR-T) is an unresolved clinical and economic question. Fixed-duration glofitamab offers a middle ground
Post-bispecific treatment: What to do after bispecific failure is poorly defined. CAR-T after bispecific exposure? Different target bispecific? This is an emerging unmet need


2. Product Profiles
2.1 EPKINLY (epcoritamab-bysp) — Genmab/AbbVie
Mechanism of Action / Delivery:
Epcoritamab is a CD20xCD3 T-cell engaging bispecific antibody administered subcutaneously. It redirects T cells to kill CD20-expressing B-cell lymphoma cells through T-cell engagement and activation. The SubQ route enables outpatient administration after the step-up dosing period and is a key competitive differentiator.
Approved Indications (as of April 2026):

R/R DLBCL, NOS (including DLBCL arising from indolent lymphoma) and HGBL after 2+ lines of systemic therapy (May 2023, accelerated approval)
R/R FL after 2+ lines of systemic therapy (Jun 2024, accelerated; converted to full approval Nov 2025)
R/R FL after 1+ line of systemic therapy in combination with R2 (Nov 2025, full approval based on EPCORE FL-1)

Key Clinical Data:

EPCORE NHL-1 (3L+ DLBCL): ORR 63%, CR 39%; mDOR 15.6 months; 3-year DoCR 36.1 months. In CAR-T exposed patients: ORR 52%, CR 35%; in CAR-T refractory: ORR 42%, CR 28%
EPCORE FL-1 (2L+ FL combination): Epcoritamab + R2 vs R2: ORR/CR 96%/~75% vs ~80%/~34%; significantly superior PFS; ~3 out of 4 patients achieved CR. Category 1 Preferred in NCCN guidelines
EPCORE DLBCL-1 (2L+ R/R DLBCL, Phase 3, Jan 2026): Epcoritamab monotherapy vs investigator's choice chemo: PFS improved (HR 0.74, 95% CI 0.60-0.92); CRR, DoR, and time to next treatment improved. OS not statistically significant (HR 0.96). This was a mixed result — PFS win but OS miss
EPCORE NHL-2 Arm 1 (1L DLBCL, Phase 1/2): Epcoritamab + R-CHOP: ORR 98%, CR 85%; 92% remained in CR at 2 years, 74% at 3 years

Safety Profile:

CRS: Predominantly low-grade, predictable, manageable with 3-step-up dosing
No ICANS (immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome) reported with 3-step-up dosing in FL
Most common AEs: CRS, fatigue, injection site reactions, musculoskeletal pain
Treat-to-progression dosing: administered once every 4 weeks after cycle 3 in DLBCL; up to 12 cycles in FL combination

Revenue:

FY2025 global net sales: $468M (+67% YoY)
Genmab net product sales (US + Japan): $379M (+50%)
Run-rate trajectory suggests ~$600-700M+ potential for FY2026

Commercial Structure:

Genmab originated the DuoBody platform and epcoritamab
AbbVie co-development and co-commercialization in US and Japan (50/50 cost sharing; AbbVie received $750M upfront + up to $3.15B milestones)
AbbVie's sales force promotes alongside Genmab; AbbVie call plan targets segmented by DLBCL/FL
Genmab records product sales; AbbVie profit-sharing recognized as cost of goods (~$46M/quarter in Q3 2025)

2.2 COLUMVI (glofitamab-gxbm) — Roche/Genentech
Mechanism / Delivery:
CD20xCD3 bispecific antibody, IV administration, fixed-duration treatment (~8.5 months, 12 cycles).
Approved Indication: R/R DLBCL, NOS or LBCL arising from FL, after 2+ lines of systemic therapy (Jun 2023). Also approved in 2L+ DLBCL based on STARGLO.
Key Data:

NP30179 (3L+ DLBCL): ORR 56%, CR 43%; mDOR 18.4 months; mDOCR not reached. Fixed-duration is a key differentiator — patients complete therapy
STARGLO (2L+ DLBCL, Phase 3): Glofitamab + GemOx vs R-GemOx: OS significantly improved (median 25.5 vs 12.9 months, HR 0.59); ORR 56% vs 43%, CR 58.5% vs 25.3%. This is the strongest 2L dataset among bispecifics in DLBCL
SKYGLO (1L DLBCL, Phase 3): Glofitamab + Pola-R-CHP vs Pola-R-CHP. Ongoing — data anticipated
CRS: Any grade 70%; Grade 1 in 52%, Grade 2 in 14%, Grade 3 in 2.8%, Grade 4 in 1.4% — higher overall CRS rate than epcoritamab but also mostly low-grade

Competitive Position:

Fixed-duration is attractive for patients and physicians who prefer a defined treatment endpoint
IV administration is a disadvantage vs epcoritamab's SubQ
STARGLO 2L OS data (HR 0.59) is the strongest Phase 3 outcome data for any bispecific in DLBCL — a direct competitive threat to epcoritamab's 2L positioning

2.3 Mosunetuzumab (Lunsumio) — Roche/Genentech
Mechanism / Delivery:
CD20xCD3 bispecific antibody, IV administration, fixed-duration.
Approved Indication: R/R FL after 2+ lines of systemic therapy (Dec 2022).
Key Data:

GO29781 (R/R FL): ORR 80%, CR 60%; mDOR 35.9 months at 37.4-month follow-up. CRS 39%, <1% led to discontinuation
Not approved for DLBCL but being investigated in combinations
CELESTIMO (Phase 3): Mosunetuzumab + lenalidomide vs R-lenalidomide in R/R FL. Direct competitor to epcoritamab + R2

Competitive Position:

First bispecific approved in FL (before epcoritamab), but as monotherapy only
FL monotherapy data is strong (CR 60%) but inferior to epcoritamab + R2 combo (CR ~75%)
CELESTIMO is the key head-to-head threat in FL — if mosunetuzumab + lenalidomide matches epcoritamab + R2, Roche maintains competitive parity
Also developing SubQ formulation (NCT04246086)

2.4 CAR-T Therapies
Yescarta (axicabtagene ciloleucel) — Gilead/Kite:

CD19-directed CAR-T; approved in 2L+ DLBCL (ZUMA-7) and 3L+ LBCL
One-time treatment; deepest responses in fit patients
In the DLBCL PET tracker (Q4 2023), YESCARTA had the highest likelihood to increase prescription (81%), well above EPKINLY (67%)

Breyanzi (lisocabtagene maraleucel) — BMS:

CD19-directed CAR-T; approved in 2L+ LBCL (TRANSFORM) and R/R FL (TRANSCEND FL)
Defined composition (CD4:CD8 ratio) with 4-1BB costimulatory domain
TRANSCEND FL: ORR 96%, CR 73% in R/R FL — directly competitive with epcoritamab + R2 in FL
MSL study: BMS MSLs notably led in "trusted partner" perception

Kymriah (tisagenlecleucel) — Novartis:

CD19-directed CAR-T; approved in 3L+ DLBCL
Declining market share; less competitive vs Yescarta/Breyanzi in current landscape

CAR-T Competitive Dynamics:

One-time treatment vs epcoritamab's continuous therapy is the fundamental value proposition debate
CAR-T manufacturing bottleneck (4-6 weeks) creates natural bispecific use case
Epcoritamab shows activity post-CAR-T (ORR 52% in CAR-T exposed), positioning it as a viable sequence after CAR-T failure
CAR-T requires specialized centers; bispecifics are more community-accessible

2.5 Other Competitors
POLIVY (polatuzumab vedotin) — Roche/Genentech:

CD79b-targeted ADC; approved in 1L DLBCL (Pola-R-CHP, POLARIX) and R/R DLBCL (with BR)
Dominant in frontline; not a direct competitor in 3L+ but contributes to share of voice
In the PET tracker, POLIVY led in personal promotion reach (56%) and unaided awareness (43%)

Odronextamab — Regeneron:

CD20xCD3 bispecific; EMA-approved for R/R DLBCL and FL (2L+); NOT FDA-approved
ORR 52%, CR 31% in R/R DLBCL (ELM-2)
Absent from US competitive dynamics but may eventually enter

Lenalidomide (Revlimid) — BMS:

Backbone of R2 combination in FL; also used with mosunetuzumab in CELESTIMO
Epcoritamab + R2 approval builds on lenalidomide's established role in FL


3. Competitive Landscape Summary
3.1 DLBCL 3L+ Market Share of Voice (ZoomRx PET Q4 2023)
Based on the Promotional Effectiveness Tracker:

POLIVY leads in personal promotion reach (56%) and unaided awareness (43%)
EPKINLY and COLUMVI tied in personal promotion reach (38%), but EPKINLY leads in unaided awareness (24% vs 15%)
EPKINLY overall call quality: 75% (competitive with COLUMVI at 76%)
EPKINLY likelihood to increase prescription: 67% (improved from prior quarter)
YESCARTA had highest prescribing intent (81%) — CAR-T's strength in conversion
EPKINLY leads COLUMVI in message association and message correct attribution

3.2 MSL Competitive Dynamics (ZoomRx MSL Wave 1, 2025)
Based on the MSL Impact Assessment:

Epcoritamab MSL reach was significantly higher vs most competitors; driven by community hospital reach
Epcoritamab MSL share of voice narrowly edged out competitors with leading reach and competitive frequency
AbbVie/Genmab MSLs drove the strongest manufacturer perceptions; rated most often as "Best in Class"
AbbVie/Genmab MSL effectiveness average: 74% (competitive with glofitamab at 75%, ahead of mosunetuzumab at 66%)
~60% of epcoritamab MSL interactions were high-impact (leading vs competitors)
AbbVie/Genmab MSLs excelled in FL scientific information discussion; ranked mid-pack for DLBCL
BMS MSLs notably led in "trusted partner" perception
Post-MSL interaction, HCPs most often discussed epcoritamab info with peers; significantly more independent research and event attendance for epcoritamab vs competitors

4.3 Genmab Broader Pipeline
Rina-S (rinatabart sesutecan): Genmab's next major pipeline asset; ADC targeting FRalpha. Phase 3 in platinum-resistant ovarian cancer (RAINFOL-02), endometrial cancer (RAINFOL-03), and platinum-sensitive ovarian cancer maintenance (RAINFOL-04, starting Jan 2026). FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation in advanced endometrial cancer. First registrational readout expected 2026 with potential 2027 launch. Not in hematology but critical to Genmab's overall portfolio diversification.

5. Genmab/AbbVie Alliance Structure
Partnership origin: June 2020 — broad oncology collaboration
Scope: Joint development and co-commercialization of epcoritamab (and two other bispecific candidates) in US and Japan
Economics:

AbbVie upfront: $750M
Milestone potential: up to $3.15B
Cost sharing: 50/50 for epcoritamab development and commercialization costs
Genmab books revenue; AbbVie profit-sharing as COGS (~$46M/quarter in Q3 2025)
Outside US/Japan: Genmab retains commercialization rights

Commercial implications:

Dual sales force (AbbVie reps + Genmab MSLs) provides broader reach
AbbVie call plan segments by DLBCL/FL designation
AbbVie brings oncology commercial infrastructure; Genmab brings innovation and hematology depth
The Q4 2023 PET tracker showed coordinated promotional activity across both partners' target lists
